results.generated_question: My pea plants are getting yellow leaves at the bottom, what's going on?
results.generated_answer: The yellowing of lower leaves in your pea plants is a sign of wilt disease. This happens because of root rot caused by fungi like Fusariumoxysporum and Rhizoctonia solani. The leaves turn yellow, and the plants may get stunted. The stems might look swollen and brittle near the soil. To manage this, treat your seeds with Carbendazim 50% WP or Captan before sowing. Also, use the right amount of fertilizer based on soil testing. You can add 8 tonnes of farmyard manure and 20 kg of nitrogen and 25 kg of phosphorus per acre. If you're planting peas for the first time in an area, treat the seeds with Rhizobiumleguminosarum to help them grow better.
results.seed_question: How can I control the yellowing of lower leaves in pea plants?
results.seed_answer: Yellowing of lower leaves in pea plants is a symptom observed in wilt disease.   Causes and Symptoms: Wilt, root rot (Fusariumoxysporum andRhizoctonia solani) Root rotting and yellowing of lower leaves, followed by wilting.  of lower leaves and stunting of plants. Wilt: Yellowing of lower leaves and stunting of plants. The xylem vessels develop brown discoloration and get distorted. Leaflet margins curl downward and inward The stem may be slightly swollen and brittle near the soil. Internal woody stem tissue often is discolored, turning lemon brown to orange brown.Externally, the root system appears healthy; however, secondary root rots are likely to occur on plants wilted for long periods. Eventually, wilted plants may die.  Management: - Avoid early sowing in badly infested areas. -  Treat the seed with 15 g Talc based formulation of Pseudomonas fluorescens per kg seed before sowing -  For wilt control, treat the seed with Carbendazim 50% WP 1 g or Captan 2 g per kg of seed.  Nutrient Management: Maintain balanced fertilization with recommended doses of NPK based on soil testing. For peas,  Apply 8 tonnes of farmyard manure,20 kg of N (45 kg Urea) and 25 kg P2O5 (155 kg Superphosphate)per acre before sowing.  In areas where pea crop has not been sown earlier,it is advisable to treat the seed with bacterial culture (Rhizobiumleguminosarum) to ensure nodule formation and quick growth. It increases the yield and quality of pods. The culture is available in the Department of Microbiology, Punjab Agricultural University, Ludhiana. One acre culture packet should be mixed with half litre of water. Rub the mixture thoroughly on seed to give a fine covering of the culture to every seed. Thereafter, spread the seed in shade for drying and sow it immediately afterwards  For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

results.seed_question: Can we use an overdose of DAP fertilizer higher than the recommended rate in maize crop?
results.seed_answer: Using an overdose of DAP (Di-Ammonium Phosphate) fertilizer beyond the recommended rate in maize crop is not advisable and can lead to several adverse effects on crop health, soil fertility, and environmental sustainability. Understanding the proper fertilizer management practices is essential for achieving optimal maize yield while maintaining soil health.   Nutrient Requirement of Maize: Maize is a heavy feeder crop that requires balanced nutrition throughout its growth cycle. The recommended dose of fertilizers for maize under irrigated condition according to varieties : - PMH 1, 11, 13, 14 & 17, DKC 9144, Bioseed 9788, ADV 9293, JC 12 and  Punjab Sweet Corn 1 : N - 50 kg/acre  P- 24 kg/acres and K -12 kg/acres. DAP- 55 kg/acres. - PMH 2, JC 4 and  Pearl Popcorn: N - 35 kg/acres , P - 12kg/acres and  K-8 kg/acres and DAP is 27 kg/acres. - To all recommended varieties, drill  entire quantity of phosphorus at the time of sowing.  - When 27 kg of DAP is used, reduce the urea dose by 10 kg and when 55 kg of DAP is used, reduce the dose of urea by 20 kg.   Consequences of DAP Overdose:    1. Nutrient Imbalance: Excessive DAP application creates an imbalance in the NPK ratio. High phosphorus levels can interfere with the uptake of micronutrients, particularly zinc and iron, leading to induced deficiencies. Maize is particularly sensitive to zinc deficiency, which manifests as interveinal chlorosis on younger leaves and stunted growth.    2. Soil Health Deterioration: Continuous overdose of phosphatic fertilizers leads to phosphorus build-up in soil, which can alter soil pH and reduce the availability of other essential nutrients. This creates long-term soil fertility issues.    3. Environmental Pollution: Excess phosphorus not utilized by plants can leach into groundwater or run off into surface water bodies, contributing to eutrophication and water quality degradation.    4. Economic Loss: Applying fertilizer beyond crop requirement represents wasteful expenditure without proportionate yield increase, reducing farm profitability.   Recommended Fertilizer Management Practices:   Soil Testing: Before any fertilizer application, farmers should conduct soil testing to determine existing nutrient status. This helps in making informed decisions about fertilizer doses tailored to specific field conditions.   Micronutrient Management: In zinc-deficient soils (common in Punjab), Where zinc deficiency had been noticed in the preceding crop, broadcast 10 kg of zinc sulphate heptahydrate (21%) or 6.5 kg zinc sulphate monohydrate (33%) per acre at sowing mixed with an equal quantity of dry soil, along rows, hoe it into the soil and then irrigate the field. In standing crop, apply 10 kg of zinc sulphate heptahydrate (21%) or 6.5 kg zinc sulphate monohydrate (33%) mixed with an equal quantity of dry soil along rows, hoe it into the soil and then irrigate the field.    When the symptoms are observed late in the season and interculture is not possible, spray zinc sulphate-lime mixture prepared by mixing 1.2 kg of zinc sulphate heptahydrate (21%) and 0.6 kg of unslaked lime or 0.75 kg zinc sulphate monohydrate (33%) and 0.38 kg of unslaked lime with 200 litres of water to cover one acre zinc sulphate @ 10 kg/acre as soil application or spray 0.5% zinc sulphate solution at 30 and 45 days after sowing.   Safety Precautions: While handling chemical fertilizers, farmers should wear protective gloves and avoid direct skin contact. Store fertilizers in cool, dry places away from food items and keep out of reach of children. Wash hands thoroughly after handling.   Conclusion: Adhering to recommended fertilizer doses based on soil testing ensures optimal maize productivity, maintains soil health, and prevents environmental contamination. Over-application of DAP or any fertilizer does not guarantee higher yields and often results in diminishing returns.   For further guidance or clarification on fertilizer management practices specific to your field conditions, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

However, I must inform you that Sultan and Rudraksh are NOT recognized wheat varieties approved by the Indian Council of Agricultural Research (ICAR) or recommended by any state agricultural universities for cultivation in Uttar Pradesh or other wheat-growing regions of India.  These names do not appear in the official list of released and notified wheat varieties maintained by the Central Sub-Committee on Crop Standards, Notification, and Release of Varieties for Agricultural Crops. Farmers should be extremely cautious about purchasing seeds marketed under unverified or non-notified variety names, as:  1. Quality Assurance: Non-notified varieties lack official testing for yield potential, disease resistance, and adaptation to local conditions.  2. Seed Authenticity: Risk of purchasing spurious or low-quality seeds  3. Financial Risk: No guaranteed performance or compensation mechanisms  4. Insurance Claims: Crop insurance may not cover losses from unapproved varieties.  Recommended Wheat Varieties for Meerut District (Western UP): For the Meerut region of Uttar Pradesh, I strongly recommend the following officially approved wheat varieties:  1-Irrigated Varieties (Timely Sowing)  For sowing typically occurring in the second fortnight of November, the following high-yielding varieties are recommended:  UP-2382 & PBW-343: These are among the highest producers, yielding 24–26 quintals per acre. They mature in 135–140 days and 125–140 days, respectively.  DBW-17: A variety resistant to Ratua (Rust), it yields 24–26 quintals per acre and matures in 125–135 days.  HD-2967: A popular high-productivity variety that yields 22–24 quintals per acre and matures in 122–125 days.  K-0307 (Shatabdi) & K-1006: Both yield approximately 22–24 quintals per acre. K-1006 is specifically noted for its resistance to both Ratua and Jhushsa diseases.  K-9107 (Dewa): This variety yields 18–20 quintals per acre and is resistant to Karnal Bunt.  2-Delayed Sowing Varieties (Irrigated)  When sowing is delayed until 25th December, specialized varieties are used to ensure maturity before extreme heat:  UP-2425: Yields approximately 16–18 quintals per acre and matures in 120–125 days.  N.W.-1076: A variety with a yield of 16–18 quintals per acre and a faster maturity of 110–115 days.  Raj-3765 & PBW-373: These varieties are also recommended for late conditions, typically yielding 14–18 quintals per acre.  3-Non-Irrigated (Rainfed) Varieties  For fields without assured irrigation, sowing is recommended from the second fortnight of October to the first fortnight of November:  Maghar (K-8027): This variety is resistant to Kanduwa and Jhushsa. It yields 12–14 quintals per acre and has a longer maturity period of 140–145 days.  Where to Purchase Genuine Seeds:  1. Government Seed Stores: UP State Seed Corporation outlets  2. NSC (National Seeds Corporation) authorized dealers  3. IFFCO/KRIBHCO cooperative societies  4. Certified Private Seed Companies: Only those with proper certification tags  Seed Quality Checks:  - Always demand certified or foundation seed with proper tagging.  - Check the seed tag for: Variety name, lot number, germination percentage (minimum 85%), physical purity (98% minimum)  - Purchase from authorized dealers with valid licenses  Action Steps:  Before purchasing any wheat variety, please contact your nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK)** in Meerut district. For further guidance on approved wheat varieties and genuine seed sources, please contact your nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) immediately before making any seed purchases.

results.seed_question: How to control aphids (chepa) in wheat crop?
results.seed_answer: Aphids, commonly known as 'chepa' in Hindi, are one of the most damaging sucking pests affecting wheat crops in Haryana and other wheat-growing regions of India. These small, soft-bodied insects can cause significant yield losses if not managed properly during the critical growth stages of wheat.   Visible Symptoms and Damage: Aphid infestation causes multiple problems in wheat crops. Infested plants show yellowing and curling of leaves, particularly on younger foliage. The pests suck plant sap, causing stunted growth and reduced tillering. Heavily infested plants develop a sticky honeydew coating on leaves, which promotes the growth of black sooty mold fungus, further reducing photosynthetic efficiency. During the grain-filling stage, aphid feeding leads to shriveled, lightweight grains with reduced quality and yield losses of 20-40% in severe cases. The pest also acts as a vector for viral diseases like Barley Yellow Dwarf Virus (BYDV).  These pests multiply rapidly under favorable conditions, with peak infestation occurring during February-March when temperatures range between 20-25°C.   Control Measures: Cultural Control: Implement several preventive measures to reduce aphid incidence. Avoid early sowing of wheat, as early-sown crops are more susceptible to aphid buildup. Remove alternate host plants and weeds from field borders. Maintain balanced fertilization, avoiding excessive nitrogen, which promotes succulent growth attractive to aphids. Ensure proper irrigation management, as water-stressed plants are more vulnerable. Grow resistant or tolerant wheat varieties like HD-2967, WH-1105, or DBW-88 that show reduced susceptibility to aphids.  Biological Control: Encourage natural enemies of aphids, including ladybird beetles (Coccinella septempunctata), lacewings (Chrysoperla carnea), and parasitic wasps (Aphidius species). These beneficial insects can provide long-term suppression of aphid populations. Avoid indiscriminate use of broad-spectrum insecticides that harm natural enemies.  Chemical Control: Give a seed treatment of Imidacloprid 48 % FS @ 0.14 kg per 100 kg of seeds. For immediate and effective control of heavy aphid populations, apply Malathion 50% EC @ 400 ml in 250 lit. of water or Thiamethoxam 25% WG @ 20 grams per acre (50 g/hectare), PHI-21 Days in 200 L of water, providing excellent systemic action. Or Quinalphos 25 % EC @ 400 ml per acre in 200-400 litres of water. Apply insecticides when aphid population reaches economic threshold level (10-15 aphids per earhead). For best results, spray during early morning or late evening hours when temperatures are cooler. Ensure thorough coverage of both upper and lower leaf surfaces. Repeat application after 10-15 days if infestation persists. Malathian 50 mg mix it in 250 liters of water and sprinkle it on the crop per acre .  For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

results.seed_question: Information regarding Fasal Bima Yojana
results.seed_answer: Pradhan Mantri Fasal Bima Yojana (PMFBY) is the Government of India’s flagship crop insurance scheme, started in Kharif 2016 to give farmers affordable insurance cover against yield loss from sowing to post‑harvest. It provides compensation to farmers for crop losses due to drought, flood, hailstorm, pests, and diseases. The scheme offers crop insurance at a very low premium and covers the crop from sowing to harvesting stages. Under PMFBY, the premium rate for Rabi crops such as wheat, mustard, and barley is 1.5%. All farmers who take crop loans through the Kisan Credit Card (KCC) scheme are eligible; earlier enrolment was compulsory for loanee farmers, but it is now voluntary, and non-loanee farmers can also apply. Farmers can apply online through the official portal pmfby.gov.in or at Common Service Centres (CSCs). Offline applications can be submitted at the Agriculture Department office, KVK or ATMA offices, and banks in the case of loanee farmers.

results.seed_question: What is the recommended seed soaking treatment for summer moong cultivation?
results.seed_answer: Seed soaking and treatment before sowing is a critical practice for successful summer moong (green gram) cultivation, particularly in Punjab's Fazilka region. This pre-sowing practice combines disease protection, biological nitrogen fixation enhancement, and improved germination rates.   Importance of Seed Treatment in Summer Moong: Summer moong, sown during March-April in Punjab, faces unique challenges including soil-borne pathogens, seed-borne diseases, and the need for efficient nitrogen fixation. Proper seed treatment addresses these challenges and establishes a strong foundation for the crop.   Recommended Seed Soaking and Treatment Protocol: - The Punjab Package of Practices (POP) recommends seed treatment with Rhizobium. Inoculate the seed with one packet of consortium biofertilizer (Rhizobium sp. LSMR-1 and Rhizobacterium RB-3) per acre at the time of sowing. Moisten the seed with about 300 mL of water, mix the seed thoroughly with the culture, and allow it to dry in the shade. Sow the seed within one hour of application of the biofertilizer. Inoculation of seed with consortium biofertilizer enhances grain yield. This biofertilizer is available at the Punjab Agricultural University Seed Shop, Gate No. 1, Ludhiana, and at Krishi Vigyan Kendras (KVKs)/Farm Advisory Service Centres in different districts.   For region-specific guidance on seed treatment protocols, Rhizobium culture procurement, recommended moong varieties for summer season, and soil testing facilities, please contact your nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK). KVK experts can provide location-specific recommendations based on your soil type and local conditions, ensuring optimal crop establishment and yield in summer moong cultivation.

results.generated_question: My paddy field has salty soil, what can I do to fix it?
results.generated_answer: To fix salty soil in your paddy field, add organic matter like paddy or wheat straw. Mix it into the soil up to 15–20 cm deep in April or May. results.seed_question: What are the effective management practices for saline soil in paddy cultivation?
results.seed_answer: Effective management of saline (kallar) soils in paddy cultivation focuses on improving soil structure, leaching excess salts, and restoring productivity. The most economical and effective practice is the regular addition of organic matter, especially crop residues like paddy straw, wheat straw, and other plant materials. Incorporating these residues into the soil improves aggregation, increases water infiltration, and helps wash salts below the root zone. During decomposition, organic matter releases organic acids that reduce the harmful effects of salts. In Punjab, including areas like Bathinda, the best time to incorporate residues is April–May after wheat harvest and before paddy transplanting. Spread residues uniformly, mix them into the soil up to 15–20 cm using a disc harrow or rotavator, and apply 2–3 irrigations to aid decomposition and salt leaching. Additional important practices include deep ploughing (30–40 cm) to break hard pans and improve drainage, green manuring with dhaincha (Sesbania) before transplanting to add organic matter and nitrogen, and proper water management by maintaining standing water in the field so salts remain dissolved and do not accumulate near the roots. Gypsum application can be used where sodium salinity is high; apply based on soil test results, preferably along with organic matter, as gypsum supplies calcium that replaces sodium and improves soil structure. Farmers should also prefer salt-tolerant paddy varieties and continue organic matter addition over multiple seasons for lasting improvement. For kallar soil reclamation and testing, dig a 3-foot-deep pit with one side vertical and the other slanting. From the vertical side, collect about ½ kg of soil by removing a 1-inch layer from depths of 0–6, 6–12, 12–24, and 24–36 inches. Keep each depth sample in a separate clean cloth bag, properly labelled with field number, depth, farmer’s name, address, and sampling date, and send for soil analysis to guide precise reclamation measures.

results.seed_question: What is the recommended fertilizer dose for sugarcane at the time of planting?

General Fertiliser Dose Recommendations: At the time of planting, sugarcane requires a balanced application of macronutrients Nitrogen (N), Phosphorus (P), and Potassium (K). The standard recommendation for sugarcane is approximately 60 kg of nitrogen (130 kg of Urea) for Planting and 90 kg of nitrogen (195 kg of Urea) for ratoon. If the soil shows deficiencies of Phosphorus and Potassium use 12 kg of phosphorus (75 kg of SSP) and 30 kg of Potassium (50 kg of Muriate of Potash) per acre for the entire crop cycle. However, the application is split across different growth stages for optimal nutrient use efficiency.   Basal Application (At Planting Time): Apply all the Phosphorus and Potash at the time of Planting. For Ratoon Crop: Top-dress one-third of the nitrogen in February with the first hoeing. Rainfed Crop: If the moisture in the soil is optimum, apply one-half of the dose of nitrogen at planting. In case the moisture is deficient, the whole dose should be applied with the onset of rains   Application Method: Fertilisers should be applied in the furrow at the time of planting, mixed thoroughly with the soil, and covered before placing the seed setts. This ensures that developing roots have immediate access to essential nutrients, particularly phosphorus, which has limited mobility in soil. Placement 5-7 cm away from the setts prevents fertiliser burn while maintaining nutrient availability.   Organic Amendments:  Apply 8 tonnes of FYM/press-mud per acre 15 days before planting and mix into the soil with a plough or tiller. In case of trench planting, apply press-mud at the base of the trench and mix it into the soil with a kasola. In case FYM/press-mud is applied, use 40 (90 kg urea) kg N per acre. However, on coarse textured soilsif FYM is applied along with the recommended dose of nitrogen, approximately 10% higher yields can be obtained. On sandy soils, nitrogenous fertiliser may be applied after irrigation in moist soil, and the number of splits may be increased. results.seed_question: What causes blackening or darkening of cotton lint/fiber and how to prevent it?
results.seed_answer: Blackening or darkening of cotton lint/fiber is a significant quality deterioration issue that affects the marketability and commercial value of cotton produce. This problem, often referred to as 'black cotton' or 'sticky cotton,' can occur due to several factors including microbial contamination, insect honeydew deposits, and improper harvesting practices.   Primary Causes of Cotton Fiber Blackness: The main cause of blackening in cotton fiber is the contamination by honeydew secretions from sucking pests, particularly aphids, whiteflies, and mealybugs. These insects feed on cotton plant sap and excrete a sugary substance called honeydew on the cotton bolls and developing fiber. This honeydew provides an ideal substrate for the growth of sooty mold fungi (primarily Cladosporium species), which appear as black or dark gray deposits on the cotton lint. Additionally, bacterial and fungal infections during boll development, especially during periods of high humidity and rainfall, can cause direct blackening of the fiber.   Other contributing factors include late-season boll opening during rainy weather, poor field hygiene, delayed harvesting, and contact with contaminated soil or plant debris. Physiological disorders due to nutrient imbalances or water stress can also predispose cotton bolls to microbial colonization and subsequent blackening.   Symptoms and Characteristics: Affected cotton shows visible black or dark brown staining on the lint surface. The fiber may appear sticky due to honeydew deposits, making it difficult to process in ginning mills. Contaminated cotton has reduced spinning quality, lower fiber strength, and significantly diminished market value. The blackening can range from light spotting to severe discoloration covering large portions of the lint.   Integrated Management Practices: Preventive Cultural Practices: Maintain proper field sanitation by removing and destroying crop residues after harvest. Follow recommended spacing to ensure good air circulation and reduce humidity within the crop canopy. Avoid excessive nitrogen application, which promotes lush vegetative growth and attracts sucking pests. Practice timely irrigation to prevent water stress while avoiding over-irrigation that creates humid conditions favorable for fungal growth. Harvest cotton promptly when bolls are fully mature and dry, preferably during dry weather conditions.   Pest Management (Primary Strategy): Effective control of sucking pests is crucial to prevent honeydew accumulation. Monitor fields regularly for aphids, whiteflies, and mealybugs. For aphid control, spray Imidacloprid 17.8% SL at 40 ml per acre or Thiamethoxam 25% WG at 40 gm mixed in 120 to 150 liters of water. For whitefly management, apply Dimethoate 30% EC @ 300 ml or Oxydemeton Methyl 25% EC @ 300 ml or 1 litre Neem based insecticide alternately in 250 litres of water per acre of crop. For mealy bug, dissolve 1.5 gm Thiodicarb 75% WP or 4 ml Quinalphos 25% EC in one litre of water and spray it only on the affected and surrounding plants and if the pest spreads to the entire field, spray it two to three times in the entire field. If there is a severe infestation of mealybugs on the crop later, then use these insecticides again on the entire crop at an interval of 5 to 6 days.   Safety Precautions: When applying any pesticide, always wear protective clothing including long-sleeved shirts, long pants, gloves, and face masks. Avoid spraying during windy conditions or peak sunshine hours. Observe the Pre-Harvest Interval for insecticides. Store pesticides in original containers in a cool, dry place away from food items and out of children's reach. Follow label instructions carefully regarding dosage and application methods.   Additional Recommendations: Use resistant or tolerant cotton varieties when available. Maintain balanced fertilization with emphasis on potassium and micronutrients to improve plant health and natural resistance. Consider biological control agents like Beauveria bassiana or Lecanicillium lecanii for sucking pest management in organic production systems.   For further guidance or clarification regarding cotton fiber blackening issues, specific pest identification, or customized management recommendations for your local conditions, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

results.seed_question: How to grow Tomato in 1 hectare of land in Punjab?

It is sensitive to frost. Under low temperature, the plant growth is restricted and fruit setting is low. The critical factor in the setting of fruits is the night temperature, the optimum range being 15–200 C.The red pigment in the fruit will develop only when the temperature is between 150 C-300 C. Above this range of temperature, only the yellow pigment formed. When the temperature exceeds 400 C, no pigment will be formed. Tomato can be grown in all types of soils, but the soil should be friable. However, it grows best in light soils ranging from sandy loam to loam. Sowing time :  For winter planting, sowing should be done in October and transplanting in November-December.  economically covered with polythene bags of 35 ×25 cm size and of 100- gauge thickness. For February planting, sow seed in the end of November and protect seedlings from frost in the nursery beds by covering with polythene sheets or sarkanda thatch. However, the yield obtained would be comparatively lower from February planted crop than from November planted crop. To ensure successful growing of healthy seedlings from costly hybrid seeds of tomato, grow nursery of this crop under polyhouse (size 24′×13′×6′) made of UV stabilized low density polyethylene microns (800 guage) thickness. Nursery prepare:- Prepare 1.5 m wide and 20 cm high beds in an area of about two marlas (50 m2) to raise seedlings for an acre. Mix 5 quintals of well rotten farmyard manure with the soil and water the beds at least 10 days before sowing Manuare:- Manures and Fertilizers : Apply 10 tonnes of well rotten farmyard manure and plough it into the soil. Add 25 kg of N (55 kg of Urea) along with 25 kg of  P2 O5 (155 kg of Single Superphosphate) and 25 kg of K2 O (45 kg of Muriate of Potash) per acre in a band at 15 cm on one side of the bed mark and prepare the channels. After removing sarkanda/polythene bag cover by mid February, apply 35 kg of N (75kg of Urea) per acre in the rows, but not touching the base of the vines. Mix it with the soil and earth up. irrigation:- 1. Furrow irrigation: First irrigation should be given immediately after transplanting. Subsequent irrigations may be given after 6-7 days during summer and 10-15 days during winter months. Total number of irrigations required is 14 to 15.  2. Drip irrigation, fertigation and plastic mulching: Drip irrigation, fertigation and silver-black plastic mulching in rainy Varieties:- 1.Punjab Ratta’ are determinate with dense & dark green foliage. The fruits are medium sized, oval, very firm suitable for processing due to deep red colour with average lycopene content of 8 mg/100 g and TSS contents of 5%,yields 220-230 q/acre while the February transplanting gives 170-180 q/acre 2.Punjab Varkha Bahar-4, First picking  is  possible 88 days after transplanting. It is suitable for cultivation in rainy/autumn season. It is resistant to leaf curl virus. Its TSS is 3.8% and  lycopene content is 3.13 mg/100g of fresh weight.  Average yield is 245 q/acre. 3.Punjab Swarna (2018),. First picking is possible 120 days after transplanting. It gives early average yield (harvested till end March) of 166 q/acre and average yield of 1087 q/acre. It is suitable for table purpose.

results.seed_question: What are the IPM practices should I follow to control Green Leaf Hoppers?
results.seed_answer: Green Leaf Hopper (GLH), also known as green rice leafhopper (Nephotettix virescens), is a significant pest of paddy that causes both direct feeding damage and serves as a vector for viral diseases like tungro. Understanding and managing this pest is crucial for maintaining healthy paddy crops.   Pest Identification and Damage Symptoms: Green Leaf Hoppers are small, wedge-shaped insects, approximately 3-4 mm long, with a characteristic green coloration. They feed by sucking sap from rice leaves, causing yellowing and drying of leaf tips (a condition called 'hopper burn'). Heavy infestations lead to stunted growth, reduced tillering, and significant yield losses. More importantly, GLH transmits Rice Tungro Virus, which causes severe economic damage to paddy crops.   Severity Assessment: Monitor your paddy fields regularly, especially 15-45 days after transplanting. Check the undersides of leaves during early morning or late evening when hoppers are most active. Initial infestations show scattered yellowing, while severe infestations display widespread leaf discoloration and plant stunting.   Control Measures:  The most effective means of GLH management is to use GLH-resistant and tungro-resistant varieties, such as IR 50, IR 54, IR 64, CR 1009, PY 3, Co 46, and White Ponni.  For Severe Infestations: Chemical control is recommended when pest population exceeds ETL level. The economic threshold level (ETL) of brown planthopper is 60 insects per 25 net sweeps, or 5 insects per hill at the vegetative stage, 10 insects per hill at the flowering stage, and 2 insects per hill in tungro-endemic areas. If ETL exceeds, spray or broadcast any one of the following insecticides twice at 15 and 30 days after transplanting per acre: Imidacloprid 17.8% SL @ 40-50 ml per acre, thiamethoxam 25% WG @ 40 g per acre, carbofuran 3% CG @ 10 kg per acre, buprofezin 25% SC @ 320 ml per acre, fipronil 5% SC @ 400- 600 ml per acre, fipronil 0.3% G @ 6.7- 10 kg per acre. Use 200-300 liters of water volume for one acre spray.  Biological control: The most abundant predator is the green mired bug Cyrtorhinus lividipennis, which predate on both eggs and nymphs.   Cultural Practices:  - Remove alternate hosts and weeds from field bunds  - Avoid excessive nitrogen fertilization, which attracts hoppers  - Maintain proper plant spacing for good air circulation  - Use light traps to monitor and reduce adult populations - Intercrop soybean in upland rice to reduce the incidence of leafhoppers in rice.  Trap Methods: - Set up light traps to attract and kill the moths.  - Set up light trap one for at least 1 acre. - Set up bird perches (16-18 /acre) of Insectivorous Bird sat vegetative phase of crop. - Monitor through pheromone traps (@ 4-5/acre for timely control measures. Change the lure at 15-20 days intervals   For further guidance or clarification, farmers are advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer.

results.seed_question: How to control armyworm, silk cutter, and motha (purple nutsedge) weed in maize crop?
results.seed_answer: This query addresses integrated pest and weed management in maize, specifically targeting armyworm, silk cutter (corn earworm), and motha weed (purple nutsedge - Cyperus rotundus). Let me address the pest management first, followed by weed control.   1-Fall armyworm: The young larvae feed by scraping the leaf surface, making papery windows. The bigger larvae feed voraciously on the central whorl leaves, causing round to oblong holes and producing a large amount of faecal matter. The larvae can be identified by a predominant white-coloured inverted Y-shaped mark on the head and the presence of four spots arranged in a square pattern at the tail end.  Adopt the following control measures: • Sow the crop at recommended time only.  • Avoid staggered sowing of maize in adjacent fields to minimize spread of this pest. • Regularly monitor the field to collect and destroy egg masses of fall armyworm from  leaves. Egg masses are covered with hairs and are easily visible. • Spray the crop with chlorantraniliprole 18.5 SC @ 0.4 mL per litre of water or emamectin benzoate 5 SG @ 0.4 g per litre of water using 120 litres of water per acre for crop up to 20 days old. For older crop, increase the spray volume to 200 litres of water per acre with corresponding increase in dosage of the above insecticides. For effective management, direct the nozzle towards the whorl.  • If the infestation is in patches or the crop is more than 40 days old and spraying is difficult, apply soil–insecticide/biopesticide mixture (about 0.5 g) in the whorls of infested plants. To prepare the mixture, add 5 mL chlorantraniliprole 18.5 SC or 5 g emamectin benzoate 5 SG in 10 mL water and mix well in 1 kg of soil, then apply in the whorl of infested plants.  2-Armyworm and silk cutter: These insects feed on the leaves in the whorl of maize plants, causing damage to the foliage and reducing plant vigour. Spray the crop 2–3 weeks after sowing, as soon as injury to the leaves is noticed, with chlorantraniliprole 18.5 SC @ 30 mL per acre using 60 litres of water per acre with a knapsack sprayer. Please contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) for proper pest identification before taking any management action, as fall armyworm, silk cutter and armyworm are important pests of maize. Correct pest identification helps in better management and avoids unnecessary crop input and pesticide use.  3- MOTHA (PURPLE NUTSEDGE) WEED CONTROL: Motha (Cyperus rotundus) is a perennial sedge weed with extensive underground tuber system, making it difficult to control.  Cultural method: Give two hoeings at about 15 to 30 days after sowing using khurpa, kasaula, wheel-hoe, triphali or tractor-drawn cultivator to control weeds. Alternatively, uniform spreading of 30 quintals of paddy straw mulch per acre at the time of sowing provides effective control of annual weeds. Another option is to grow one or two rows of cowpea between maize rows and harvest it as fodder 35 to 45 days after sowing; after that, no additional weed control operation is required. For intercropping of cowpea, use 8 kg seed per acre of variety CL 367. It does not require any additional fertilizer. Sow maize and cowpea simultaneously.  Spray tembotrione 34.4% SC @ 105 mL per acre in 150 litres of water at 20 days after sowing for effective control of mixed weed flora. For the control of dila/motha, apply 2,4-D amine salt 58 SL @ 400 mL per acre as post-emergence at 20–25 days after sowing in 150 litres of water.  For further guidance on pest identification, Economic Threshold Level determination, selection of appropriate pesticides, resistant varieties, or clarification on integrated management practices, farmers are strongly advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer in Tarn Taran district. KVK experts can provide field-specific recommendations based on pest intensity and crop stage.

results.seed_answer: This query addresses integrated pest and weed management in maize, specifically targeting armyworm, silk cutter (corn earworm), and motha weed (purple nutsedge - Cyperus rotundus). Let me address the pest management first, followed by weed control.   1-Fall armyworm: The young larvae feed by scraping the leaf surface, making papery windows. The bigger larvae feed voraciously on the central whorl leaves, causing round to oblong holes and producing a large amount of faecal matter. The larvae can be identified by a predominant white-coloured inverted Y-shaped mark on the head and the presence of four spots arranged in a square pattern at the tail end.  Adopt the following control measures: • Sow the crop at recommended time only.  • Avoid staggered sowing of maize in adjacent fields to minimize spread of this pest. • Regularly monitor the field to collect and destroy egg masses of fall armyworm from  leaves. Egg masses are covered with hairs and are easily visible. • Spray the crop with chlorantraniliprole 18.5 SC @ 0.4 mL per litre of water or emamectin benzoate 5 SG @ 0.4 g per litre of water using 120 litres of water per acre for crop up to 20 days old. For older crop, increase the spray volume to 200 litres of water per acre with corresponding increase in dosage of the above insecticides. For effective management, direct the nozzle towards the whorl.  • If the infestation is in patches or the crop is more than 40 days old and spraying is difficult, apply soil–insecticide/biopesticide mixture (about 0.5 g) in the whorls of infested plants. To prepare the mixture, add 5 mL chlorantraniliprole 18.5 SC or 5 g emamectin benzoate 5 SG in 10 mL water and mix well in 1 kg of soil, then apply in the whorl of infested plants.  2-Armyworm and silk cutter: These insects feed on the leaves in the whorl of maize plants, causing damage to the foliage and reducing plant vigour. Spray the crop 2–3 weeks after sowing, as soon as injury to the leaves is noticed, with chlorantraniliprole 18.5 SC @ 30 mL per acre using 60 litres of water per acre with a knapsack sprayer. Please contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) for proper pest identification before taking any management action, as fall armyworm, silk cutter and armyworm are important pests of maize. Correct pest identification helps in better management and avoids unnecessary crop input and pesticide use.  3- MOTHA (PURPLE NUTSEDGE) WEED CONTROL: Motha (Cyperus rotundus) is a perennial sedge weed with extensive underground tuber system, making it difficult to control.  Cultural method: Give two hoeings at about 15 to 30 days after sowing using khurpa, kasaula, wheel-hoe, triphali or tractor-drawn cultivator to control weeds. Alternatively, uniform spreading of 30 quintals of paddy straw mulch per acre at the time of sowing provides effective control of annual weeds. Another option is to grow one or two rows of cowpea between maize rows and harvest it as fodder 35 to 45 days after sowing; after that, no additional weed control operation is required. For intercropping of cowpea, use 8 kg seed per acre of variety CL 367. It does not require any additional fertilizer. Sow maize and cowpea simultaneously.  Spray tembotrione 34.4% SC @ 105 mL per acre in 150 litres of water at 20 days after sowing for effective control of mixed weed flora. For the control of dila/motha, apply 2,4-D amine salt 58 SL @ 400 mL per acre as post-emergence at 20–25 days after sowing in 150 litres of water.  For further guidance on pest identification, Economic Threshold Level determination, selection of appropriate pesticides, resistant varieties, or clarification on integrated management practices, farmers are strongly advised to contact the nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) or local agricultural extension officer in Tarn Taran district. KVK experts can provide field-specific recommendations based on pest intensity and crop stage.

results.seed_question: How to manage yellow leaves (chlorosis/panda) in wheat crop?
results.seed_answer: Yellow leaves or 'panda' in wheat is a common nutrient deficiency symptom, primarily caused by lack of zinc, iron, or nitrogen in the soil. In Uttar Pradesh, particularly in districts like Kaushambi, alkaline and calcareous soils often lock up micronutrients, making them unavailable to plants. This deficiency typically appears during the tillering to jointing stage of wheat and can significantly reduce yields if not addressed promptly.   Symptom Identification: Yellowing (chlorosis) in wheat manifests differently based on the deficient nutrient. Zinc deficiency shows interveinal chlorosis on younger leaves, with green veins and yellow tissue between them. Leaves may develop brown necrotic spots and plants appear stunted with reduced tillering. Iron deficiency causes uniform yellowing of young leaves while older leaves remain green. Nitrogen deficiency shows uniform paleyellow color starting from older leaves and progressing upward, with overall weak plant growth.  Corrective Management: If the symptom of deficiency of zinc appears in standing crop, spray 2 kg zinc sulphate and 6 kg urea dissolved in 320 liter water per acre. If the top dressing of urea is already done, spray zinc sulphate dissolved in 1 kg lime water in place of urea (dissolve 1 kg quick lime in 4 liter water in the evening and filter it in the morning and use filtered water and through away the lime).Please note that it is essential to use urea or lime water with zinc sulphate. If zinc sulphate has not been used in paddy field as basal dressing and there is possibility of deficiency, top dress with 8-10 kg zinc sulphate.    Soil Application for Long-term Management: For sustained correction and prevention in subsequent crops, apply 10 kg Zinc Sulphate per acre mixed with soil or farmyard manure before sowing. In severely deficient soils, combine this with 10 kg Ferrous Sulphate per acre. Soil application is more economical and provides lasting benefits for 2-3 crop cycles.   Integrated Nutrient Management: Ensure balanced fertilization with recommended NPK doses for wheat in UP (48 kg Nitrogen, 24 kg Phosphorus, 16 kg Potassium per acre). Split nitrogen application - 50% at sowing, 25% at first irrigation (CRI stage, 21 days), and 25% at second irrigation helps maintain green foliage. Incorporate well-decomposed farmyard manure (4-4.8 tonnes/acre) to improve soil structure and micronutrient availability.   Preventive Measures:  - Test soil regularly (every 2-3 years) to identify nutrient deficiencies  - Treat wheat seeds with zinc-enriched solutions before sowing  - Avoid excessive use of phosphatic fertilizers which can induce zinc deficiency  - Maintain proper soil pH (6.5-7.5) through judicious use of amendments  - Ensure adequate irrigation but avoid waterlogging which reduces nutrient uptake   Precautions During Spraying: Wear protective clothing, gloves, and face mask while preparing and applying spray solution. Do not eat, drink, or smoke during application. Wash hands and exposed body parts thoroughly after spraying. Keep children and livestock away from freshly sprayed fields for at least 24 hours.   Important Note: If yellowing persists despite corrective measures, it could indicate other issues like disease infection, waterlogging, or salinity stress. For further guidance and soil testing facilities, please contact your nearest Krishi Vigyan Kendra (KVK) in Kaushambi district or the District Agriculture Office.
